title: Study on the control effect and mechanism of Serratia rubidaea B11 metabolites on postharvest blue mold of pear fruit.
author_string: Wang, Kaili, Pan, Tengxia, Wang, Hua, Godana, Esa Abiso, Lu, Yuchun et al.
abstract: Postharvest pear fruit are highly susceptible to pathogen infection, leading to decay. Antagonistic bacteria control postharvest diseases of fruits and vegetables through the production of secondary metabolites that inhibit or kill pathogens. This study investigated the efficacy of Serratia rubidaea B11 against blue mold (Penicillium expansum) in postharvest pears. Non-volatile metabolites (cell-free filtrate) produced by S. rubidaea B11 showed no significant control effect on pear blue mold. In contrast, volatile organic compounds (VOCs) emitted by this strain effectively suppressed mycelial growth and sporulation of P. expansum, demonstrating significant biocontrol efficacy against the disease. GC-MS analysis identified the major constituents of these VOCs, with 2-octanone identified as a key antifungal compound. Notably, this is the first report of 2-octanone production by S. rubidaea. Fumigation with 2-octanone at 0.04 μL/mL significantly reduced the decay incidence and lesion diameter (p < 0.05) in pear fruit, with complete disease suppression achieved at concentrations ≥0.08 μL/mL. At ≥0.04 μL/mL, 2-octanone fully inhibited spore germination and germ tube development in P. expansum. This study highlights the potential of S. rubidaea B11 VOCs, particularly 2-octanone, for controlling postharvest blue mold in pears, providing important insights for developing environmentally friendly and sustainable alternatives to chemical fungicides.
